summary:
The Execution of Charles I (January 30, 1649 AD) was an unprecedented act in European history — the public trial and beheading of an anointed king of England by his own subjects — that shocked monarchs throughout Europe and opened the first significant experiment with republican government in a major European state. Following the defeat of the Royalist cause in the [[event:2327|English Civil War]], a 'Rump Parliament' (purged of moderate members by Colonel Pride's Purge in December 1648) established a High Court of Justice to try Charles I for treason against his own people — a charge grounded in the idea that sovereignty rested with the people and Parliament, not the king.

Charles I refused to acknowledge the court's legitimacy ('I do not believe that the pretended authority being set up here of the Commons of England... was ever a legal authority'), conducting himself with considerable dignity. He was condemned and executed before a large crowd outside the Banqueting House at Whitehall on January 30, 1649. His final speech from the scaffold — 'I go from a corruptible to an incorruptible Crown' — framed his death as martyrdom; Anglican hagiography quickly elevated him to 'King Charles the Martyr' (still commemorated in the Church of England on January 30). The immediate consequences were the abolition of the monarchy and House of Lords, the declaration of a Commonwealth, and then [[event:2408|Oliver Cromwell's Protectorate]]. Monarchy was restored under Charles II in 1660, but the execution permanently established that English kings governed under law and by consent — a principle confirmed by the [[event:2330|Glorious Revolution]] 39 years later.
